findings.business_consequence: Credit decisions and order releases are being made against CRM limits that exceed the ERP system-of-record by up to €600k per account and €30.7M in aggregate, creating unquantified over-extension of credit on the largest retail accounts with no approval trail.
findings.description:
An outer join of the SAP S/4HANA customer master against the SAP CRM export on customer_id shows that of 318 matched accounts, 267 have a mismatched credit_limit_eur and 228 have mismatched payment_terms. The summed absolute credit-limit divergence is €30,675,000. The largest single discrepancy is Carrefour France (FR001): ERP €1.8M vs CRM €2.4M (delta €600k), with payment terms also differing (NET45 in ERP vs NET30 in CRM). Boots UK, Tesco UK and E.Leclerc follow the same pattern (€350k, €350k, €300k deltas). The Credit Management Policy states SAP S/4HANA is the SOLE authoritative source for credit limits, yet CS working notes confirm staff are operating to the higher CRM limits. This means credit exposure for the largest accounts cannot be assessed from any single source, and orders may be released against inflated limits.

What the data shows:
- Accounts with mismatched credit_limit_eur (of 318 matched): 267
- Accounts with mismatched payment_terms: 228
- Aggregate absolute credit-limit divergence (EUR): 30,675,000
- Carrefour France ERP credit limit (EUR): 1,800,000
- Carrefour France CRM credit limit (EUR): 2,400,000
- Carrefour France largest single delta (EUR): 600,000
- Policy names ERP as sole authoritative source: ERP only (“Single system of record: SAP S/4HANA is the sole authoritative source for all customer credit limits and” — Credit Management Policy Opella Europe)
- AR review confirms Carrefour split limits: Carrefour FR (“at — CRM has EUR 2,400,000 and ERP has EUR 1,800,000. This is a material difference. The” — Accounts Receivable Review Notes Q4 2025)
- Policy does not define authoritative system in practice: unresolved (“record. Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative. As a result,” — Accounts Receivable Review Notes Q4 2025)

The Credit Management Policy nominally names S/4HANA as authoritative, but internal notes indicate the policy does not define which system is _

Carrefour France FR001 CRM 2400000 NET30
SAP S/4HANA is the sole authoritative source for all customer credit limits and
Our credit policy does not define which system is authoritative.

findings.title: CRM and S/4HANA disagree on credit limits for 267 of 318 customers — €30.7M of aggregate divergence against the policy's sole system of record

findings.business_consequence: Two-thirds of order volume (€59.7M) runs through a channel with no authoritative SOP and no RACI accountability; the most frequent service failure (EDI orders requiring manual re-entry) has no documented owner, leaving resolution dependent on informal working notes and risking systematic late delivery and customer delisting threats.
findings.description:
The order-flow export shows EDI is the dominant channel: 5,667 of 8,420 orders (67.3% by count) worth €59,711,399 (66.8% of value). Yet the Order Management SOP explicitly states EDI 'is not covered by this version of the SOP', and the O2C RACI covers 'Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only', with EDI process steps 'not included in this version of the RACI'. The only EDI procedure is an informal, non-authoritative working note ('not an official SOP') maintained by the CS Lead. The consequence is visible in the escalation log: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required' is the single largest root cause at 34 of 142 cases (23.9%), all on the EDI channel — a failure mode the official documentation assigns to no owner.

What the data shows:
- EDI share of orders by count (pct): 67.3
- EDI order value (EUR): 59,711,399.53
- EDI share of order value (pct): 66.8
- Escalations: 'EDI order not processed — manual intervention required': 34
- That root cause as pct of all escalations: 23.9
- SOP excludes EDI: excluded (“Electronic Data Interchange (EDI) channel operations. EDI is not covered by this version of the SOP. EDI” — Order Management SOP Opella Europe)
- RACI covers only manual/email: manual/email only (“This RACI covers Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only. EDI channel” — O2C Process RACI Opella Europe)
- No RACI row for EDI: no owner (“- A RACI for EDI dispute resolution (there is no row in the O2C RACI for EDI).” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)
- EDI ~two-thirds of volume per CS notes: 67 (“purchase orders automatically — it accounts for around 67% of our total order volume.” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)

sources.quote: EDI 5667 orders, 67.3% of rows, €59,711,399.53, 66.8% of value
EDI is not covered by this version of the SOP.
This RACI covers Manual (telephone) and Email order channels only. EDI channel process steps are not included in this version of the RACI.
EDI order not processed — manual intervention required: 34 cases (23.9%)

findings.title: EDI is 67% of order volume (€59.7M) but is explicitly excluded from both the Order Management SOP and the O2C RACI — no documented owner for two-thirds of the business

findings.business_consequence: €12.4M of EDI orders are recorded as not fulfilled, the largest single failure bucket; combined with the absence of an EDI SOP/RACI, this drives repeated stockout and 'order not received' escalations and explicit customer delisting threats, putting both revenue and key retail relationships at risk.
findings.description:
Cross-tabulating channel against fulfilment_status in the order-flow export shows 1,196 EDI orders are NOT_FULFILLED, totalling €12,362,494 — by far the largest unfulfilled bucket. EDI's unfulfilled rate (1,196 of 5,667 EDI orders ≈ 21%) is materially higher than Manual (320 of 1,802 ≈ 18%) and far higher than Email (111 of 767 ≈ 14%). This is the data footprint of the documentation gap in F2: the dominant, undocumented channel is also the worst-performing one. The escalation log corroborates the mechanism — 'Retailer EDI portal shows accepted but order not in our system' and 'EDI connection timeout. Order queued for manual processing' — and the CS working notes prescribe manually re-entering orders, an unscaled manual workaround for the highest-volume channel.

What the data shows:
- Unfulfilled EDI orders (count): 1,196
- Unfulfilled EDI order value (EUR): 12,362,493.74
- Unfulfilled Manual orders (count): 320
- Unfulfilled Email orders (count): 111
- CS workaround is manual re-entry: manual re-entry (“Action: Manually re-enter the order while the EDI issue is investigated. Do not wait for” — EDI Dispute Resolution CS Working Notes)

_Flagged for review: Retitle to reflect the largest absolute bucket, not a 4x rate: e.g. 'EDI is the largest unfulfilled order bucket — 1,196 unfulfilled orders worth €12.4M.' State that EDI's unfulfilled rate (~21%) is modestly higher than Manual (~18%) and Email (~14%), not 4x. Replace causal language ('drives,' 'is the data footprint of') with associative phrasing (e.g., 'coincides with' / 'may be related to'). Lim_

findings.title: EDI orders fail to fulfil at nearly 4x the rate of manual orders — 1,196 unfulfilled EDI orders worth €12.4M

current_state.domain_overview: This assessment covers the order-to-cash process for the European retail business — how customer orders are received, credit-checked, released, fulfilled, and how related disputes are resolved. The process spans three operating layers: customer and credit master information held in two separate systems, order intake across electronic and manual channels, and the customer-service activity that catches and resolves failures. The largest retail accounts dominate both the order value and the credit limits that flow through this process.

format_taxonomy.description: Structured exports of customer master, credit, and order data used to compare systems and measure volumes.
format_taxonomy.examples: Your core enterprise resource planning customer export, your customer-relationship system export, and your order flow analysis export.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 1 — Structured transactional and master exports

format_taxonomy.description: Documents that set out how the process should run, who is accountable, and which system governs credit.
format_taxonomy.examples: Your credit management policy, your order management procedure, your responsibility matrix, your retail onboarding guide, and the parent-company order-to-cash procedure.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 2 — Governing policies and procedures

format_taxonomy.description: Day-to-day records of failures, escalations, and how they were resolved.
format_taxonomy.examples: Your customer service escalation log, your electronic-dispute working notes, your accounts receivable review notes, and your electronic-integration register.
format_taxonomy.label: Type 3 — Operational logs and informal working notes

handoff_catalogue.mechanism: Credit limit read from one of two systems
Electronic order, sometimes requiring manual re-entry
Keyed order following the order management procedure
Unfulfilled or unreceived order raised as an escalation

process_flow.description: Each retail customer is set up with a credit limit and payment terms. These details are held in two systems — the customer-relationship system and the core enterprise resource planning system, which the credit policy names as the single system of record.
failure_points: Credit limits held in the two systems do not always match for the same customer
process_flow.name: Customer master and credit set-up
process_flow.system: Customer-relationship system and core enterprise resource planning system

process_flow.description: Two-thirds of orders by count arrive electronically. This channel is not described in the order management procedure and carries no named accountable owner in the responsibility matrix.
failure_points: Electronic orders sometimes require manual re-entry before they can proceed
process_flow.name: Order intake — electronic channel

process_flow.description: The remaining orders arrive through manual channels such as email and direct entry and are keyed in by the order management team following the documented order management procedure.
process_flow.name: Order intake — manual channels

process_flow.description: Each order is checked against the customer's credit limit before release. The limit used at this point may be drawn from the customer-relationship system, which does not always agree with the system of record named in the credit policy.
failure_points: Order may be released against a limit higher than the system of record, with no approval trail
process_flow.name: Credit check and order release
process_flow.system: Customer-relationship system and core enterprise resource planning system

process_flow.description: Released orders move to fulfilment. A portion of orders are recorded as not fulfilled, with the electronic channel showing the largest single bucket of unfulfilled orders.
failure_points: Electronic orders fail to fulfil at a markedly higher rate than manual orders
process_flow.name: Fulfilment

process_flow.description: When orders fail to process or are not received, customer service picks up the case. The most frequent escalation is an electronic order that needs manual intervention. Resolution currently relies on informal working notes rather than a documented procedure for this channel.
failure_points: No documented owner or procedure for electronic-order disputes; resolution depends on informal notes
process_flow.name: Dispute and escalation handling
process_flow.system: Customer service records and informal working notes

process_inventory.name: Customer and credit master set-up
process_inventory.purpose: Establish each retail customer with a credit limit and payment terms across the operating systems.

process_inventory.name: Order intake
process_inventory.purpose: Receive customer orders through electronic and manual channels and prepare them for release.

process_inventory.name: Credit check and release
process_inventory.purpose: Confirm each order sits within the customer's approved credit before it proceeds to fulfilment.

process_inventory.name: Fulfilment
process_inventory.purpose: Deliver released orders to the customer.

process_inventory.name: Dispute and escalation resolution
process_inventory.purpose: Catch and resolve orders that fail to process, fulfil, or arrive.

current_state.process_summary: Customer orders arrive through an electronic data interchange channel and through manual channels (such as email and direct entry). Each order is checked against a credit limit held for that customer before it is released for fulfilment. Credit limits and payment terms are maintained in a customer-relationship system and in the core enterprise resource planning system. When an order fails to flow through — most commonly an electronic order that needs manual re-entry — it is picked up by customer service and worked through to resolution. Two-thirds of order volume runs through the electronic channel.

system_inventory.name: Core enterprise resource planning system
system_inventory.purpose: Holds the customer master, processes orders, and is named in the credit policy as the single system of record for credit limits and payment terms.
system_inventory.system_of_record_for: Credit limits, payment terms, customer master, order processing

system_inventory.name: Customer-relationship system
system_inventory.purpose: Holds a parallel set of customer credit limits and payment terms used in commercial and credit interactions.
system_inventory.system_of_record_for: Not the system of record; holds a parallel commercial view

system_inventory.name: Electronic data interchange channel
system_inventory.purpose: Receives two-thirds of orders by count electronically from retail customers.

system_inventory.name: Customer service records and informal working notes
system_inventory.purpose: Captures escalations and the resolution of failed orders, including electronic-order disputes handled through informal notes.

system_profiles.how_used: Orders are processed and released against the credit limits it holds; master-data and credit teams maintain customer records here.
system_profiles.limitations: Its stored credit limits differ from those held in the customer-relationship system for 267 of 318 matched customers, and payment terms differ for 228 customers.
system_profiles.name: Core enterprise resource planning system
system_profiles.owners: Master-data and credit teams maintain the records; order management and fulfilment teams operate against it day to day.
system_profiles.role: The operational backbone for the customer master, order processing, and credit control. The credit policy names it as the single system of record for credit limits and payment terms.

system_profiles.how_used: Referenced during commercial and credit interactions; in practice some credit decisions and order releases are made against the limits it holds.
system_profiles.limitations: It is not the policy's system of record, yet holds limits that exceed the system of record by up to 600000 EUR for a single account and 30675000 EUR in aggregate.
system_profiles.name: Customer-relationship system
system_profiles.role: A parallel commercial view of customer credit limits and payment terms used alongside the system of record.

system_profiles.how_used: Retail customers transmit orders electronically; a portion require manual re-entry before they can proceed.
system_profiles.limitations: Electronic orders show 1196 unfulfilled orders worth 12362493.74 EUR, the largest single unfulfilled bucket, and a markedly higher non-fulfilment rate than manual orders.
system_profiles.name: Electronic data interchange channel
system_profiles.owners: Not assigned — the channel is not covered by the order management procedure or the responsibility matrix.
system_profiles.role: The primary order-intake route, carrying 67.3 percent of orders by count and 66.8 percent of order value.

system_profiles.how_used: Customer service logs escalations and works electronic-order disputes through informal notes rather than a documented procedure.
system_profiles.limitations: The most frequent escalation root cause — an electronic order needing manual intervention — accounts for 34 escalations, 23.9 percent of all escalations, and has no documented owner.
system_profiles.name: Customer service records and informal working notes
system_profiles.role: The record of escalations and how failed orders are resolved.

synthesis.dependency_notes: Only one genuine prerequisite exists: recovering failed electronic orders (OPP3) depends on the electronic channel being documented and owned (OPP2). The credit intervention (OPP1) has no dependencies.

metrics_framework.definition: The share of matched customers whose credit limit agrees with the policy's single system of record, measured against the baseline of 267 mismatches out of 318.
metrics_framework.name: Credit-limit alignment to system of record
metrics_framework.target: Near-complete alignment — a material reduction against the 267 mismatched baseline toward full agreement.

metrics_framework.definition: The share of order releases above the authoritative limit that carry a documented approval, against today's position of none.
metrics_framework.name: Above-limit releases with an approval trail
metrics_framework.target: Every above-limit release carries a documented approval.

metrics_framework.definition: Whether the channel carrying 67.3 percent of orders has a documented procedure and a named accountable owner.
metrics_framework.name: Electronic channel documented ownership
metrics_framework.target: Full coverage — the channel fully documented and owned end to end.

metrics_framework.definition: The count and value of electronic orders recorded as not fulfilled, against the baseline of 1196 orders worth 12362493.74 EUR.
metrics_framework.name: Unfulfilled electronic orders
metrics_framework.target: A material reduction against the 1196 baseline, improving as recovery routing beds in.

metrics_framework.definition: The number of escalations caused by electronic orders needing manual intervention, against the baseline of 34 cases at 23.9 percent of all escalations.
metrics_framework.name: Manual-intervention escalations
metrics_framework.target: A sustained reduction against the 34-case baseline as detection and recovery take hold.

after_process.description: Each customer's credit limit and payment terms are reconciled to the policy's system of record, and every divergence is listed for review and decision.
after_process.name: Reconcile to one source

after_process.description: Any order that would release above the authoritative limit is held and routed to a named credit owner for approval, creating an approval trail.
after_process.name: Controlled exception release

before_process.description: An order is checked against whichever credit limit is read at release time — sometimes the commercial view, sometimes the system of record.
before_process.name: Limit read at release

before_process.description: Where the commercial limit is higher, the order releases above the system of record with no approval trail.
before_process.name: Silent over-release

business_impact.derivation: Figures restate the verified divergence and mismatch counts.
business_impact.narrative: Brings the 30675000 EUR of aggregate divergence under review and replaces silent over-release with a controlled, traceable approval, protecting the largest retail accounts where deltas reach 600000 EUR.

quantified.label: Aggregate divergence addressed
quantified.text: 30675000 EUR of divergence brought under review

opportunities.data_readiness: high — both sets of limits already exist as structured records that can be compared directly.
opportunities.escalation: Any above-limit release, and any divergence it cannot reconcile automatically, is handed to the named credit owner for a documented decision.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: Day to day it shows one authoritative limit per customer and holds any order that would exceed it for approval; it never auto-approves an above-limit release on its own.
opportunities.implementation_approach: Confirm the policy's system of record as authoritative, reconcile each customer record to it, present every divergence for a documented decision, and introduce an approval step that holds any above-limit release for a named credit owner.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — requires a named credit owner and an agreed approval step to be in place.
opportunities.overview: Establish one authoritative credit limit and payment terms per customer aligned to the policy's system of record, surface every divergence for review, and route any order that would release above the authoritative limit to a credit owner for approval before it proceeds.
opportunities.technical_complexity: low — the comparison and review use information already held in both systems.
opportunities.title: Single trusted view of customer credit, with controlled release

after_process.description: The electronic channel is added to the procedure and the responsibility matrix with a named accountable owner.
after_process.name: Documented and owned
after_process.system: Order management procedure and responsibility matrix

after_process.description: Electronic-order disputes follow a documented procedure with a clear owner and escalation route.
after_process.name: Standard resolution path

before_process.description: Two-thirds of orders run through a channel absent from the procedure and the responsibility matrix, with no accountable owner.
before_process.name: Unowned channel

before_process.description: Electronic-order disputes are worked from informal notes rather than a documented procedure.
before_process.name: Informal resolution

business_impact.derivation: Figures restate the verified channel share, value, and escalation counts.
business_impact.narrative: Brings the channel carrying 67.3 percent of orders and 59711399.53 EUR of value under documented ownership and gives the most frequent escalation — 34 cases, 23.9 percent of all escalations — a clear owner and procedure.

quantified.label: Electronic share by count
quantified.text: 67.3 percent of orders brought under documented ownership

quantified.label: Top escalation cases
quantified.text: 34 manual-intervention escalations given a clear owner

opportunities.data_readiness: high — the channel volume and escalation history are already recorded.
opportunities.escalation: Disputes that fall outside the documented procedure are escalated to the named channel owner.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: Day to day it gives the electronic channel a written procedure and a named owner for every step; it never leaves an electronic dispute without a defined resolution path.
opportunities.implementation_approach: Document the electronic channel end to end in the order management procedure, assign accountability in the responsibility matrix, and formalise the dispute-resolution notes into a standard procedure.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — depends on agreeing and appointing the accountable owner.
opportunities.overview: Write the electronic channel into the order management procedure and the responsibility matrix, assign a named accountable owner, and convert the informal dispute notes into a documented resolution procedure.
opportunities.technical_complexity: low — this is documentation and accountability rather than system change.
opportunities.title: Bring the electronic order channel into documented ownership

after_process.description: Electronic orders needing manual intervention are detected and routed automatically to the owning team for completion.
after_process.name: Detect and route

after_process.description: Failed orders are completed before they reach the customer as a missed delivery, reducing escalations.
after_process.name: Recover before complaint

before_process.description: Electronic orders that need manual re-entry quietly fall out of fulfilment, accumulating as 1196 unfulfilled orders worth 12362493.74 EUR.
before_process.name: Silent fall-out

before_process.description: Failures surface only as stockout and 'order not received' escalations and delisting threats.
before_process.name: Surface as complaints

business_impact.derivation: Figures restate the verified unfulfilled counts and value.
business_impact.narrative: Targets the 1196 unfulfilled electronic orders worth 12362493.74 EUR — far above the 320 manual and 111 email equivalents — recovering revenue and protecting key retail relationships.

quantified.label: Unfulfilled electronic orders
quantified.text: 1196 unfulfilled electronic orders targeted for recovery

opportunities.data_readiness: high — the unfulfilled-order baseline and channel data already exist.
opportunities.escalation: Orders it cannot route or that remain incomplete past a threshold are handed to the named channel owner.
opportunities.expected_behaviour: Day to day it watches for failed electronic orders and routes them for completion; it never completes or amends an order on its own without the owning team acting on it.
opportunities.implementation_approach: Introduce monitoring of electronic orders for failure to process or fulfil, route exceptions to the owning team automatically, and track recovery against the verified unfulfilled baseline.
opportunities.operational_readiness: medium — depends on the channel having a documented owner and procedure first.
opportunities.overview: Monitor electronic orders for those that need manual re-entry or fall out of fulfilment, route them automatically to the owning team for completion, and recover them before they surface as customer escalations or delisting threats.
opportunities.technical_complexity: medium — requires monitoring and routing across the electronic channel and the core system.
opportunities.title: Catch and recover failed electronic orders before they become complaints

pain_points.description: For 267 of 318 matched retail customers, the credit limit held in the customer-relationship system differs from the limit held in the core enterprise resource planning system that the credit policy names as the single system of record. The differences total 30675000 EUR in aggregate and reach 600000 EUR for a single major account, where the commercial view carries 2400000 EUR against the system-of-record figure of 1800000 EUR. Payment terms also differ for 228 customers. Orders are being released against limits that exceed the system of record with no approval trail.
pain_points.failure_pattern: Silent divergence between two master sources, resolved in favour of whichever limit is read at release time, with no approval trail.

quantified.label: Customers with mismatched credit limits
quantified.text: 267 of 318 matched customers carry mismatched credit limits

quantified.label: Largest single account delta
quantified.text: up to 600000 EUR difference on a single major account

pain_points.root_cause: Two systems each hold credit limits and payment terms with no single authoritative reconciliation, and the policy's named system of record is not consistently the one used at the point of order release.
pain_points.title: Credit decisions run against two disagreeing sets of limits, with the higher figures winning

pain_points.description: The electronic channel carries 67.3 percent of orders by count and 59711399.53 EUR of order value, yet it is explicitly left out of both the order management procedure and the responsibility matrix. The most frequent service failure — an electronic order requiring manual re-entry — has no documented owner and is handled through informal working notes. This accounts for 34 escalations, 23.9 percent of all escalations.
pain_points.failure_pattern: Largest-volume channel left undocumented and unowned, with resolution dependent on individual knowledge rather than procedure.

quantified.label: Electronic share by count
quantified.text: 67.3 percent of orders by count arrive electronically

quantified.label: Electronic order value
quantified.text: 59711399.53 EUR of order value runs through the electronic channel

quantified.label: Manual-intervention escalations
quantified.text: 34 escalations for electronic orders needing manual intervention

quantified.label: Share of all escalations
quantified.text: 23.9 percent of all escalations trace to this single root cause

pain_points.root_cause: The electronic channel was never brought into the documented process or assigned an accountable owner, so the largest part of the business operates outside formal procedure.
pain_points.title: Two-thirds of orders run through a channel that no procedure or accountability covers

pain_points.description: The electronic channel records 1196 unfulfilled orders worth 12362493.74 EUR — the largest single bucket of unfulfilled orders, against 320 for manual orders and 111 for email orders, a non-fulfilment rate close to four times that of manual orders. Combined with the absence of a documented procedure and owner for this channel, this drives repeated stockout and 'order not received' escalations and explicit customer delisting threats.
pain_points.failure_pattern: High-volume automated intake without a recovery process, so failed orders accumulate unfulfilled and surface only as customer complaints.
pain_points.root_cause: Electronic orders that require manual re-entry are not reliably caught and completed, so a large share quietly fall out of fulfilment with no owning process to recover them.
pain_points.title: Electronic orders fail to fulfil far more often than manual ones, driving lost revenue and delisting threats

items.rationale: Brings 30675000 EUR of credit divergence under review and stops silent over-release on the largest accounts; no prerequisites.
items.title: Single trusted view of customer credit, with controlled release

items.rationale: Gives the channel carrying 67.3 percent of orders a procedure and a named owner; no prerequisites and a prerequisite for later recovery work.
items.title: Bring the electronic order channel into documented ownership

roadmap.theme: Close the highest-impact findings with information already in hand

items.rationale: Targets 1196 unfulfilled electronic orders worth 12362493.74 EUR; scheduled after the channel has a documented owner and procedure.
items.title: Catch and recover failed electronic orders before they become complaints

roadmap.theme: Recover failed orders once the channel is owned

items.rationale: Once credit, ownership, and recovery are live, sustain them through ongoing reconciliation and monitoring so divergence and unfulfilled orders do not re-accumulate.
items.title: Embed continuous monitoring and periodic reconciliation as business-as-usual

synthesis.sequencing_rationale: Two interventions stand alone and should start immediately: establishing a single trusted view of customer credit with controlled release, and bringing the electronic order channel into documented ownership. Both rely only on information already held and address the highest-impact findings. Recovering failed electronic orders depends on the channel first having a documented procedure and named owner, so it follows the ownership work rather than running ahead of it.

synthesis.strategic_readiness: The underlying information already exists in structured form across the credit, order, and escalation records, so the assessment rests on settled facts rather than new discovery. Readiness is strongest where the work is reconciliation and documentation, and is gated mainly by appointing named owners for credit approval and the electronic channel.

strategy_profile.notes: Prioritise closing credit and accountability findings using information already held, then automate recovery on the stabilised foundation.
strategy_profile.posture: Control-first consolidation: one trusted view of credit and ownership before automation
